content.text: Establishing consistent coding conventions for LLM SDK usage is the difference between a maintainable agentic platform and a collection of ad-hoc scripts that each interact with model APIs in subtly incompatible ways. Enterprise teams that standardise how they initialise clients, structure prompts, handle responses, and inject dependencies find that onboarding, code review, and incident response all become dramatically faster.

Agentic AI SDK coding conventions are a team-agreed set of patterns and rules that govern how engineers write code that calls LLM provider APIs (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI GPT, Google Gemini, etc.), manages conversation state, structures tool definitions, and wires agentic components together. They cover naming, module layout, client lifecycle, prompt construction, response handling, and testing seams.

Unlike general Python style guides (PEP 8, Black, isort), GenAI SDK conventions address concerns that are unique to working with LLMs: the difference between a system prompt and a user turn, how to handle streaming versus blocking responses, when to use structured outputs, and how to pass tool results back to the model. A living conventions document, enforced through linting rules and code review, ensures these patterns are applied uniformly.

Without agreed conventions, each engineer makes independent decisions: one creates the SDK client inside every function call, another makes it a global singleton, a third passes it through a dependency injection framework. When a rate-limit incident occurs at midnight, the on-call engineer discovers that the retry logic lives in three different places and works differently in each. Debugging time doubles.

Conventions also protect against security and cost regressions. A convention that mandates a maximum token budget parameter on every API call prevents runaway cost from a single prompt change. A convention that requires all system prompts to be sourced from versioned template files prevents unreviewed prompt changes from reaching production. These guardrails are only reliable when they are universally applied.
New engineers understand any module quickly because all LLM interactions follow the same structure. There is only one way to create a client, one way to call the model, one way to handle errors.
Conventions that mandate dependency injection over global client singletons make every LLM-calling function unit-testable without network access.
Required max_tokens and explicit model version pins prevent token budget overruns from accidental defaults.
When the SDK releases a breaking change, a single well-defined adapter layer absorbs the change rather than requiring edits across dozens of call sites.
A centralised client wrapper is the right place to attach logging, metrics, and tracing — once, for all callers.

Conventions impose a learning curve. Engineers accustomed to calling the SDK directly must learn the wrapper API, and they may initially find the extra abstraction layer friction. Over-engineering the wrapper layer is a real risk: a thin adapter that adds logging and retry is valuable; a complex framework that reimplements half the SDK is a maintenance burden.

Conventions also become outdated. When the SDK adds a new parameter or feature, the convention document must be updated in sync. Teams that treat the conventions document as immutable will find engineers routing around it. Assign ownership of the conventions document and include it in SDK upgrade checklists.

The conventions are organised into four layers: client lifecycle, call construction, response handling, and testing seams. Each layer has a canonical pattern that all code in the organisation follows.

layer defines how SDK clients are created and injected. The canonical pattern is: one client per application context, created at startup from environment variables, passed to all downstream functions as an explicit parameter. Clients are never created inside loops or per-request code paths.

layer defines how messages arrays, system prompts, tool definitions, and model parameters are assembled. System prompts come from versioned template files, never from f-strings in business logic. The model name and max_tokens are always explicit, never relying on SDK defaults.

layer defines how to extract text, tool calls, and stop reasons from API responses. The convention is always to check stop_reason before accessing content, and to treat unexpected stop reasons as structured errors rather than silently returning empty strings.
layer defines how code is structured to be testable without live API calls: all LLM client access flows through a typed protocol that can be swapped for a mock in tests.

attrs.alt: Four convention layers: Client Lifecycle, Call Construction, Response Handling, Testing Seams — each with key rules, enforced by lint and review

content.text: Apply these conventions from the first line of production LLM code. The cost of retrofitting them onto an existing codebase grows with codebase size; establishing them early costs almost nothing. For a greenfield project, the conventions document should be created before the first PR is merged.
that makes LLM API calls, regardless of team or language. Partial adoption creates the same inconsistency problem as no adoption.
from strict enforcement, but require convention-compliant code before any notebook code graduates to a production service.
whenever a major SDK version is released or a new LLM provider is added to the approved list.
class) with the minimum interface all LLM functions require. This becomes the type of the injected client parameter.
module that wraps the SDK client, attaches logging/metrics, and exposes the protocol interface. All production code uses this wrapper, never the raw SDK client.
as Jinja2 or plain text files, tracked in version control. Load them via a
that renders the template with validated variables.
Write a flake8/ruff custom rule or a pre-commit hook that flags any direct instantiation of the SDK client class outside
Add a code review checklist item for each convention layer to the PR template.
Anti-pattern: Direct SDK usage scattered through business logic

# BAD: Multiple anti-patterns in one function
import anthropic
import os

def summarise_document(text: str) -> str:
    # Anti-pattern 1: client created inside the function on every call
    client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

    # Anti-pattern 2: model hardcoded with no max_tokens
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        # Anti-pattern 3: no max_tokens — relies on SDK default
        messages=[
            # Anti-pattern 4: f-string mixing business logic with prompt text
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Summarise this document:\n\n{text}"}
        ]
    )
    # Anti-pattern 5: no stop_reason check, silent empty string on non-end_turn
    return response.content[0].text

# llm/client.py — the organisation's single wrapper module
from __future__ import annotations
import logging
from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@runtime_checkable
class LLMClientProtocol(Protocol):
    """Typed interface for LLM clients — real SDK or test mock."""
    def create_message(
        self,
        *,
        system: str,
        messages: list[dict],
        model: str,
        max_tokens: int,
        tools: list[dict] | None = None,
    ) -> anthropic.types.Message: ...


class AnthropicClientWrapper:
    """Production wrapper: attaches logging and metrics."""

    def __init__(self, *, api_key: str, default_model: str) -> None:
        self._client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.default_model = default_model

    def create_message(
        self,
        *,
        system: str,
        messages: list[dict],
        model: str | None = None,
        max_tokens: int,
        tools: list[dict] | None = None,
    ) -> anthropic.types.Message:
        resolved_model = model or self.default_model
        logger.info(
            "llm.request",
            extra={"model": resolved_model, "max_tokens": max_tokens},
        )
        kwargs = dict(
            model=resolved_model,
            system=system,
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
        )
        if tools:
            kwargs["tools"] = tools
        response = self._client.messages.create(**kwargs)
        logger.info(
            "llm.response",
            extra={
                "stop_reason": response.stop_reason,
                "input_tokens": response.usage.input_tokens,
                "output_tokens": response.usage.output_tokens,
            },
        )
        return response

# prompts/summarise.txt  (versioned in git alongside business logic)
You are a precise document summariser. Produce a concise summary of the
following document in {{ max_sentences }} sentences or fewer. Focus on
the main argument, key facts, and conclusions. Do not add interpretation
not present in the source text.

Document:
{{ document_text }}

# summarise/service.py — convention-compliant business logic
from pathlib import Path
from jinja2 import Template
from llm.client import LLMClientProtocol
import anthropic

_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE = Template(
    (Path(__file__).parent.parent / "prompts" / "summarise.txt").read_text()
)

class SummarisationError(Exception): ...

def summarise_document(
    text: str,
    client: LLMClientProtocol,   # injected — never created here
    *,
    max_sentences: int = 5,
    model: str = "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
) -> str:
    system = _SYSTEM_PROMPT_TEMPLATE.render(
        max_sentences=max_sentences,
        document_text=text,
    )
    response = client.create_message(
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Please summarise the document above."}],
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    if response.stop_reason != "end_turn":
        raise SummarisationError(
            f"Unexpected stop_reason: {response.stop_reason!r}"
        )
    return response.content[0].text

A financial services firm operates twelve microservices that each call Claude for different tasks: document extraction, customer query routing, compliance summarisation, and alert generation. Before adopting conventions, a quarterly SDK upgrade required changes in 47 different files across 9 services. After wrapping all LLM calls through a shared

package, the next upgrade required changes in exactly 1 file. Token budget overruns (previously occurring 2-3 times per quarter as engineers forgot to set max_tokens) dropped to zero after the wrapper made max_tokens a required keyword argument with no default.

The firm's conventions document specifies five rules: (1) always use the wrapper client, (2) always pass max_tokens explicitly, (3) always check stop_reason before returning, (4) always load system prompts from template files, (5) always inject the client rather than creating it. A ruff plugin enforces rules 1 and 5 automatically. Rules 2–4 are in the PR review checklist.

inside a function that is called in a web request handler means a new HTTPX connection pool is created on every request. At moderate traffic (50 req/s), this generates thousands of short-lived connections and may exhaust file descriptors. Create the client once at application startup and inject it.
Relying on SDK defaults for max_tokens
SDK defaults change between versions. Code that omits
silently changes behaviour — and cost — on SDK upgrades. Always pass an explicit value calibrated to the task.

Prompts built with f-strings in business logic cannot be reviewed by prompt specialists, cannot be tested independently, and cannot be diffed clearly in code review. A one-line change to an f-string prompt buried inside a 200-line service file can silently change model behaviour in production. Extract all prompt text to template files.
. Unpinned aliases change behavior without code changes and bypass change management.
(input_tokens, output_tokens, model, stop_reason) from the wrapper. This data is essential for cost attribution and anomaly detection.
Use structured outputs (JSON mode) for any response that will be parsed programmatically
. Free-form text parsing is brittle; structured outputs fail fast and are easier to validate with Pydantic.
— when a convention changes, document the old and new patterns, the reason, and the migration guide. Engineers on other teams need this to update their code.
that verify the wrapper's response handling against a real API call on a scheduled basis. This catches any drift between the mock used in unit tests and the live SDK.

The wrapper adds a negligible latency overhead — a few microseconds of Python function call and logging — compared to the tens to hundreds of milliseconds of the LLM API call itself. The performance-critical decision is client lifecycle management.
and create it at application lifespan startup (the
context manager in FastAPI). The async client reuses the HTTPX connection pool across requests, enabling connection multiplexing and reducing TCP handshake overhead under load.

For high-throughput batch processing (thousands of documents), use the async client with a semaphore to bound concurrency at the level that stays within rate limits rather than issuing all requests simultaneously and triggering retries.
— linting. Write custom rules (ruff supports AST-based plugins) to enforce client creation conventions and flag raw SDK imports in business logic modules.
— validate structured LLM outputs. Define response models for every endpoint that returns parsed LLM data. Pydantic's ValidationError surfaces schema mismatches immediately.
— prompt templating. Provides variable injection, conditional blocks, and loop constructs. Works well for system prompts that vary by role or context.
— run linting hooks before every commit. Catches convention violations before they enter the PR, reducing review friction.
— attach span attributes (model, tokens, stop_reason) to every LLM call via the wrapper. Provides distributed tracing across multi-service agentic pipelines.

The wrapper is the single place API keys are accessed. Convention: all API keys come from environment variables, never from config files or code. The wrapper validates at startup that required keys are present, failing fast rather than failing mid-request.
The wrapper can enforce an organisation-wide maximum max_tokens ceiling (e.g. 4096 for interactive, 16384 for batch), rejecting calls that exceed it before they hit the API.
The wrapper can optionally pass all prompt text through a PII scanner before logging. This prevents user data from appearing in log aggregation platforms.

Using template files with explicit variable insertion (Jinja2) rather than string concatenation provides a clear separation between trusted prompt text and untrusted user input. The untrusted content appears in clearly delimited sections and cannot override system instructions by its placement in the string.
Think of the SDK conventions as the

in a commercial building. Every outlet, breaker, and conduit follows the same standard (gauge, colour coding, grounding). Any qualified electrician can safely work anywhere in the building because the pattern is consistent. When a new appliance is added, it plugs into the existing standard infrastructure. When the electrical system needs to be upgraded, the upgrade path is clear and affects known, documented points.

Without wiring standards, each room was wired differently by whoever happened to do it, with no documentation. An electrician called in to fix a problem must reverse-engineer the local wiring pattern before touching anything. SDK conventions provide the equivalent standard: any engineer can work on any LLM-calling service because the pattern is always the same.

A thin wrapper around the SDK client, enforcing logging and a typed Protocol, is the highest-leverage convention: it centralises observability, enables mocking, and isolates SDK upgrade impacts to one file.
Always inject clients via parameters; never create them inside business logic functions or request handlers.
Always pass explicit model names (pinned versions) and explicit max_tokens; rely on no SDK defaults.
Store all system prompt text in versioned template files; never construct prompts with f-strings in business logic.
Always check stop_reason before returning; unexpected stop reasons are errors, not empty strings.
Enforce conventions with automated linting rules and PR checklist items, not just documentation.
Assign ownership of the conventions document and tie its review to SDK upgrade cadence.
